name: Moncayo Climbs Loop via Tarazona and Veruela
name_original: Subida a Fuente de Los Frailes por Tarazona — circular por el Moncayo desde Vera
description: This difficult 52.6-mile road cycling loop around Moncayo offers challenging climbs, historical towns, and diverse natural scenery.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Vera De Moncayo are set against the backdrop of the majestic Moncayo Mountain, offering a diverse environment for cyclists. The region features varied terrain, transitioning from dry areas to lush beech and oak forests, with routes winding through vineyards. Roads are generally in good condition with low traffic, providing a suitable setting for road cycling. Best road cycling routes around Vera De Moncayo The most popular road cycling route is Climb to Fuente de Los Frailes via Tarazona — circular route around Moncayo from Vera, a 52.6 miles (84.7 km) trail that takes 4 hours 41 minutes to complete. This difficult route offers challenging climbs around the Moncayo region. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is The Balcony of El Buste — circular from the Tarazona Cathedral, a moderate 68.1 miles (109.6 km) path. This route provides scenic views and may pass through the region's vineyards. Local road cyclists also love the Santa Cruz de Moncayo – Bar de Moncayo loop from Tarazona, a 47.0 miles (75.7 km) trail leading through the Moncayo region, often completed in about 4 hours 12 minutes. Road cycling around Vera De Moncayo is defined by the imposing Moncayo Mountain, varied terrain including beech forests, and routes through vineyards. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate to difficult. The routes in Vera De Moncayo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from more than 10 reviews. More than 1000 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Vera De Moncayo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available in Vera De Moncayo, and what are their difficulty levels?
A: Vera De Moncayo offers over 50 road cycling routes. You'll find a good mix, with around 8 easy routes, 30 moderate routes, and 15 challenging routes for more experienced cyclists.

Q: Are there easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Vera De Moncayo?
A: Yes, there are several easy road cycling routes in Vera De Moncayo. These routes often feature the region's generally good road conditions and low traffic, making them suitable for beginners or those looking for a relaxed ride. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38190253" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tarazona – Cunchillos loop from Tarazona&lt;/a&gt; is an easy option.

Q: What are some challenging road cycling routes for experienced riders in Vera De Moncayo?
A: Experienced riders seeking a challenge will find several difficult routes, often involving climbs around the Moncayo Mountain. A notable option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e988544215/climb-to-fuente-de-los-frailes-via-tarazona-circular-route-around-moncayo-from-vera" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Climb to Fuente de Los Frailes via Tarazona — circular route around Moncayo from Vera&lt;/a&gt;, which covers over 84 km with significant elevation gain.

Q: Can I find circular road cycling routes around Vera De Moncayo?
A: Yes, many road cycling routes in Vera De Moncayo are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e989999574/the-balcony-of-el-buste-circular-from-the-tarazona-cathedral" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Balcony of El Buste — circular from the Tarazona Cathedral&lt;/a&gt;, offering scenic views as you loop back.

Q: Which road cycling routes offer the best scenic viewpoints in the region?
A: The region around Moncayo Mountain is rich in scenic beauty. Routes that ascend towards the mountain or pass through areas like the beech forests of Peña Roya offer stunning vistas. You can also find routes with views of the Moncayo summit itself, such as those passing near &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5304172" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Moncayo&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5291398" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cabezo de la Mata&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What kind of elevation can I expect on road cycling routes in Vera De Moncayo?
A: Given its location at the foot of Moncayo Mountain, you can expect varied elevation. Routes range from relatively flat sections to significant climbs. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20151146" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Buste Viewpoint – Castillo de Vozmediano loop from Tarazona&lt;/a&gt; features over 2,200 meters of elevation gain, while easier routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38190253" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tarazona – Cunchillos loop from Tarazona&lt;/a&gt; have less than 200 meters.

Q: Are there family-friendly road cycling routes in Vera De Moncayo?
A: While specific 'family-friendly' designations aren't always available, the region's 8 easy routes, combined with generally good road conditions and low traffic, can be suitable for families. Look for shorter, less elevated loops to ensure an enjoyable experience for all.

Q: What is the best season for road cycling in Vera De Moncayo?
A: The diverse climate around Moncayo Mountain means cycling is enjoyable across several seasons. Spring and autumn generally offer pleasant temperatures and beautiful scenery, with lush beech forests in autumn. Summer can be warm, but higher elevations provide cooler air. Winter can be cold, especially at altitude.

Q: Are there less crowded road cycling routes in Vera De Moncayo?
A: Vera De Moncayo is often described as a tranquil escape, and its roads are reported to have very little traffic. This means many routes, even popular ones, tend to be less crowded than in more urbanized areas, offering a peaceful cycling experience.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Vera De Moncayo?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Cyclists often praise the stunning backdrop of the Moncayo Mountain, the diverse terrain transitioning from dry areas to lush forests, and the well-maintained roads with minimal traffic, which contribute to a pleasant and safe riding experience.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural attractions along the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, the region is rich in history and culture. While cycling, you might pass by historical sites like the 12th-century Cistercian Monastery of Veruela, which also houses the Wine Museum of the Campo de Borja Designation of Origin. Nearby towns like Tarazona, with its Mudejar architecture, also offer cultural excursions.

Q: Can I find routes that pass through vineyards or wine regions?
A: Absolutely. The 'Garnacha Wine Route' winds through villages like Vera de Moncayo, offering routes amidst vineyards. You can experience the unique landscape of the Campo de Borja Designation of Origin while cycling, with routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e989999574/the-balcony-of-el-buste-circular-from-the-tarazona-cathedral" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Balcony of El Buste — circular from the Tarazona Cathedral&lt;/a&gt; potentially passing through these areas.
tips:
Visiting it is doing a deep review of the history of art in Spain, since in its construction there are spaces where all the styles that have existed since the 12th century, the time when construction began, come together.  It is a unique example of a Gothic cathedral with an exceptional Mudejar and Renaissance legacy.

The Tarazona Cathedral was built in the French Gothic style, but it maintains magnificent examples of the Mudejar style, including a Gothic-Mudejar cloister.

The ascent to the Fuente de los Frailes (1,334 masl) is very popular among road bike lovers. The climb is very enjoyable along a road in very good condition with wonderful views between trees and shade. It was climbed from the Vuelta España

We are in the monastery of Santa María de Veruela, a magical place in the province of Zaragoza. The monastery combines Romanesque, Gothic, Renaissance and Baroque architecture and is surrounded by a wall. We can also visit the cloister or the exhibition of the Bécquer brothers since they worked and stayed here. Admission is free for children up to 11 years old (€2 for adults) but it is totally worth it if we are interested in the whole story.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete the Moncayo Climbs Loop via Tarazona and Veruela?
faqs.answer: This challenging road cycling loop is designed to be completed in approximately 4 hours and 41 minutes of active cycling time. However, given its difficulty and the scenic highlights, you might want to allow extra time for stops.

faqs.question: What is the starting point for this cycling loop and where can I park?
faqs.answer: While the route can be started from various points, Tarazona is a common and convenient starting location, offering good access and amenities. You'll find parking options within Tarazona, a historical town that serves as an excellent base for exploring the region.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route primarily features well-maintained roads, especially on the significant climbs. The Moncayo ascent itself has steady gradients, typically between 4% and 5%. Traffic is generally minimal on the climbs. While most of the route is paved, be aware that some 'hard' classified routes in the wider Moncayo area might include short unpaved sections, though this specific loop is predominantly road cycling.

faqs.question: What are the main scenic highlights along the Moncayo Climbs Loop?
faqs.answer: You'll experience diverse scenery, from the majestic Moncayo mountain and the forests of Moncayo Natural Park to historical towns. Key highlights include the unique Cathedral of Saint Mary of the Huerta of Tarazona, the serene Fuente de los Frailes, and the impressive 12th-century Monastery of Santa María de Veruela. The route also passes through picturesque wine country.

faqs.question: When is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Moncayo region offers beautiful cycling conditions during spring and autumn, when temperatures are milder and the natural landscapes are vibrant. Summer can be quite warm, especially in the lower sections, while winter may bring colder temperatures and potential snow at higher elevations on Moncayo.

faqs.question: Are there any specific regulations or permits required for cycling in the Moncayo Natural Park?
faqs.answer: Generally, road cycling on established public roads within the Moncayo Natural Park does not require special permits or entrance fees. However, always respect local signage, stay on designated routes, and be mindful of wildlife and other park users. For specific off-road or mountain biking regulations, it's advisable to check with local park authorities.

faqs.question: Is the Moncayo Climbs Loop via Tarazona and Veruela dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: This route is primarily a road cycling loop, which means it's not typically designed for accompanying dogs. While dogs might be allowed in certain outdoor areas, the sustained nature of the ride and potential traffic make it unsuitable for pets to join on the bike. If you plan to visit historical sites, check their individual pet policies.

faqs.question: What level of fitness is required for this challenging loop?
faqs.answer: This loop is classified as 'difficult' and requires a very good level of fitness. With over 1,400 meters of elevation gain spread across approximately 84.7 kilometers, it involves significant and sustained climbing, making it best suited for experienced road cyclists.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other cycling paths or trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route intersects with several other paths. Notably, it covers significant sections of the [CIMA ZA05] Moncayo * Tarazona (25%) and [CIMA ZA05] Moncayo * Monasterio de Veruela (17%). You'll also find it briefly crosses parts of the Camino Jacobeo Castellano-Aragonés: Borja-Tarazona (12%) and the Camino de la Vera Cruz (El camino de los Templarios) (8%).

faqs.question: Are there places to find food or accommodation along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, both Tarazona and the vicinity of the Monastery of Santa María de Veruela offer options for cafes, restaurants, and accommodation. Tarazona, being a larger town, provides a wider range of services, making it a good spot for a break or an overnight stay.

faqs.question: What makes this particular Moncayo cycling route notable?
faqs.answer: This loop is notable for combining the physical challenge of climbing the iconic Moncayo mountain, a climb featured in the Vuelta a España, with rich cultural immersion. It takes you through the historical city of Tarazona and past the architecturally significant Monastery of Santa María de Veruela, all while traversing the varied natural landscapes of the Moncayo Natural Park.

waypoints.name: Cathedral of Saint Mary of the Huerta of Tarazona
